subject: Chemistry
q:
Drug $X$ becomes ineffective after $50 \%$ decomposition. The original concentration of drug in a bottle was $16 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ which becomes $4 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ in 12 months. The expiry time of the drug in months is __________$\qquad$
Assume that the decomposition of the drug follows first order kinetics.
question:
Drug $X$ becomes ineffective after $50 \%$ decomposition. The original concentration of drug in a bottle was $16 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ which becomes $4 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$ in 12 months. The expiry time of the drug in months is __________$\qquad$
Assume that the decomposition of the drug follows first order kinetics.
solution:
Drug $\mathrm{X} \xrightarrow{\text { 1st order }}$ Product
Initial concentration of drug $=16 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$
Concentration of drug after 12 months $=4 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{mL}$
Half life of drug = 6 months
Drug become ineffective after 50\% decomposition. The expiry time of drug $=6$ months

subject: Chemistry
q: Decreasing order of dehydration of the following alcohols is
question: Decreasing order of dehydration of the following alcohols is
solution:
The dehydration of alcohol takes place via the formation of a carbocation. Hence, the rate of reaction depends on the stability of carbocation.

No dehydration is possible in phenol because carbocation formation is difficult in case of phenol.

Option (d) fast due to loss of H2O and conjugation increasing stable of compound as compared to (c). Option(b) is very fast because stable compound formed.

question: Very pure hydrogen $(99.9 \%)$ can be made by which of the following processes?

Reaction of salt like hydrides with water
solution: Highly pure hydrogen is obtained by the electrolysis of water.

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of molccules with zero dipole moment among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>BF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>HF</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ______.
question: The total number of molccules with zero dipole moment among <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CH</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>BF</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>HF</mi><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>SO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ______.
solution:
Dipole moment:- The polarity of a covalent bond can be conveniently measured in terms of a physical quantity called dipole moment. The dipole moment is the vector sum of all the bond moments. The molecules with perfect geometries with zero lone pairs have zero dipole moment.

Amongst the given compounds, 

 CO2,  BF3, CH4 have zero dipole moments.

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the chemical reaction $\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Y},$ the standard reaction Gibbs energy depends on temperature $T$ (in $K$ )

as $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}\left(\right.$ in $\left.\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)=120-\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{~T}$

The major component of the reaction mixture at $\mathrm{T}$ is :
question:
For the chemical reaction $\mathrm{X} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Y},$ the standard reaction Gibbs energy depends on temperature $T$ (in $K$ )

as $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}\left(\right.$ in $\left.\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)=120-\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{~T}$

The major component of the reaction mixture at $\mathrm{T}$ is :
solution:
$\mathrm{At} 315 \mathrm{~K} ; \Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=120-\frac{3}{8} \mathrm{~T}$

$\Delta G^{\circ}=120-118.125=$ positive

Since $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$ is positive then $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{eq}}  < 1$

So $\frac{[\mathrm{Y}]}{[\mathrm{X}]} < 1$

$\therefore[\mathrm{X}]>[\mathrm{Y}]$

subject: Chemistry
q: Green chemistry in day-to-day life is in the use of:
question: Green chemistry in day-to-day life is in the use of:
options: Large amount of water alone for washing clothes
Liquified <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for dry cleaning of clothes
solution:
Chlorine gas was used earlier for bleaching paper. These days, hydrogen peroxide H2O2 is used with suitable catalyst.

Tetrachloroethene Cl2C=CCl2 was earlier used as solvent for dry cleaning. The compound contaminates the ground water and is also a suspected carcinogen. Replacement of halogenated solvent by liquid CO2 will result in less harm to groundwater. Hence, given statement (4) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Consider the following sequence of reactions

The major product P is:
question:
Consider the following sequence of reactions

The major product P is:
solution:
Step 1: The first reagent is Cu at $573\text{ K}$. When a tertiary alcohol like tert-butyl alcohol is passed over heated copper at $573\text{ K}$, it undergoes dehydration rather than dehydrogenation to form an alkene.

$(CH_3)_3C-OH \xrightarrow{Cu / 573\text{ K}} CH_2=C(CH_3)_2 + H_2O$
The intermediate product is isobutylene (2-methylpropene).

Step 2: The second set of reagents is $H^+$ and $PhCOOH$ (benzoic acid). This is an acid-catalyzed addition of a carboxylic acid to an alkene, which is a standard method for synthesizing tert-butyl esters.
First, the alkene is protonated by the acid to form the most stable carbocation, which is the tertiary butyl carbocation:
$CH_2=C(CH_3)_2 + H^+ \rightleftharpoons (CH_3)_3C^+$

Next, the carboxylic acid acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbocation. The oxygen atom of the benzoic acid attacks the tert-butyl carbocation, followed by deprotonation:
$PhCOOH + (CH_3)_3C^+ \rightleftharpoons Ph-CO-O^+(H)-C(CH_3)_3 \rightleftharpoons Ph-CO-O-C(CH_3)_3 + H^+$

The final major product is tert-butyl benzoate. Electrophilic aromatic substitution (Friedel-Crafts alkylation) on the benzene ring does not occur here because the $-COOH$ group is strongly deactivating, making O-alkylation (ester formation) the highly preferred pathway.

Comparing this with the given options, Option (C) represents the structure of tert-butyl benzoate.

Answer:

Reason R : enol form of acetyl acetone is stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding, which is not possible in enol form of acetone.

Choose the correct statement:
question:
Assertion A : Enol form of acetone CH3COCH3 exists in <0.1% quantity. However, the enol form of acetyl acetone CH3COCH2OCCH3 exists in approximately 15%quantity.

Reason R : enol form of acetyl acetone is stabilized by intramolecular hydrogen bonding, which is not possible in enol form of acetone.

Choose the correct statement:
options: A is false but R is true
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
solution:
Acetyl acetone in enol form have intramolecular H-bonding, which is absent in acetone.

enol form of Acetyl acetone is stabilised by conjugation and by intramolecular H-bonding.

enol form of acetone is very less <0.1%

subject: Chemistry
q: If shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is $x$, then longest wavelength in Balmer series of $\text{He}^+$ is:
question: If shortest wavelength of hydrogen atom in Lyman series is $x$, then longest wavelength in Balmer series of $\text{He}^+$ is:
solution:
For the shortest wavelength in the Lyman series of the hydrogen atom ($Z=1$), the transition is from $n_2 = \infty$ to $n_1 = 1$.

$\dfrac{1}{x} = R(1)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2}\right) = R$

This gives $R = \dfrac{1}{x}$.

For the longest wavelength in the Balmer series of $\text{He}^+$ ($Z=2$), the transition is from $n_2 = 3$ to $n_1 = 2$.

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R(2)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{3^2}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 4R \left(\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 4R \left(\dfrac{5}{36}\right) = \dfrac{5R}{9}$

Substituting $R = \dfrac{1}{x}$:

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{5}{9x}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{9x}{5}$

Answer: $\dfrac{9x}{5}$

subject: Chemistry
q: Assuming that the degree of hydrolysis is small, the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium acetate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.0 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ will be:
question: Assuming that the degree of hydrolysis is small, the $\mathrm{pH}$ of $0.1 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium acetate $\left(\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}=1.0 \times 10^{-5}\right)$ will be:
solution:
Sodium acetate is a salt of strong base and weak acid.
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\therefore \mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{p} K_a+\frac{1}{2} \log c \quad \text { where } \\
&\mathrm{p} k_a=-\log K_a=-\log 10^{-5}=5 \\
&\log \mathrm{c}=\log 10^{-1}=-1 \\
&\mathrm{pH}=7+\frac{5}{2}-\frac{1}{2}=9.0
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Chemistry
q:
2.2 g of nitrous oxide N2O gas is cooled at a constant pressure of 1 atm from 310 K to 270 K causing the compression of the gas from 217.1 mL to 167.75 mL. The change in internal energy of the process, △U is '-x'J. The value of 'x' is _____.

[nearest integer]

(Given: atomic mass of N=14 g mol-1 and of O=16 g mol-1. Molar heat capacity of N2O is 100 JK-1 mol-1)
question:
2.2 g of nitrous oxide N2O gas is cooled at a constant pressure of 1 atm from 310 K to 270 K causing the compression of the gas from 217.1 mL to 167.75 mL. The change in internal energy of the process, △U is '-x'J. The value of 'x' is _____.

[nearest integer]

(Given: atomic mass of N=14 g mol-1 and of O=16 g mol-1. Molar heat capacity of N2O is 100 JK-1 mol-1)
solution:
Molecular weight of nitrous oxide \(\left(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)=44 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\) Number of moles of \(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}=\frac{2.2}{44} \quad 0.05 \mathrm{~mol}\) Specific heat capacity of \(\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}=880 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{g} . \mathrm{K}\) \(\therefore\) Heat release on cooling from \(310 \mathrm{~K}\) to \(270 \mathrm{~K}\) \(q=-2.2 \times 880 \times 40 \mathrm{~K}\) \(q=-77.44 \mathrm{KJ}\) Negative sign indicates heat released. \(\therefore\) Work done by surrounding on system to compares the gas from \(217.1 \mathrm{ml}\) to \(167.75 \mathrm{ml}\) at constant pressure. It means work done by surrounding is irreversible. \(\begin{aligned} & \therefore W= P \times \Delta V \\ & =1.01 \times 10^5 \times(167.75 \quad 217.1 \mathrm{ml} l) \\ & =1.01 \times 10^5 \times 0.049 J \\ & W=4.95 k . J \end{aligned}\) Using 1st Law of thermodynamics \(\begin{aligned} & \Delta U=q+W \\ & \Delta U=-77.44 K J + 4.95 K J \\ & \Delta U=-72.49 K J \end{aligned}\).
$\therefore x = 72.$

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements is incorrect for antibiotics?
question: Which of the following statements is incorrect for antibiotics?
options: An antibiotic must be a product of metabolism.
An antibiotic is a synthetic substance produced as a structural analogue of naturally occurring antibiotic.
An antibiotic should promote the growth or survival of microorganisms.
An antibiotic should be effective in low concentrations.
solution: An antibiotic should not promote growth or survival of microorganisms such as bacteria, fungi, algae because antibiotics are chemicals that kill or inhibit the growth of bacteria and are used to treat bacterial infections. They are produced in nature by soil bacteria and fungi.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which statements are True?
A. In Hoffmann bromamide degradation, $4$ moles of NaOH and $2$ moles of Br$_2$ are consumed per mole of an amide
B. Hoffmann bromamide reaction is not given by alkyl amides.
C. Primary amines can be synthesized by Hoffmann bromamide degradation.
D. Secondary amide on reaction with Br$_2$ and NaOH will give secondary amine.
E. The by-products of Hoffmann degradation are Na$_2$CO$_3$, NaBr and H$_2$O.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Which statements are True?
A. In Hoffmann bromamide degradation, $4$ moles of NaOH and $2$ moles of Br$_2$ are consumed per mole of an amide
B. Hoffmann bromamide reaction is not given by alkyl amides.
C. Primary amines can be synthesized by Hoffmann bromamide degradation.
D. Secondary amide on reaction with Br$_2$ and NaOH will give secondary amine.
E. The by-products of Hoffmann degradation are Na$_2$CO$_3$, NaBr and H$_2$O.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The balanced chemical equation for Hoffmann bromamide degradation is:

$\text{R-CONH}_2 + \text{Br}_2 + 4\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{R-NH}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + 2\text{NaBr} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

Statement A is false because $1$ mole of $\text{Br}_2$ and $4$ moles of $\text{NaOH}$ are consumed per mole of amide.

Statement B is false because primary alkyl amides readily undergo this reaction.

Statement C is true as the primary product of this reaction is a primary amine.

Statement D is false because the reaction is strictly given by primary amides ($\text{R-CONH}_2$), not secondary amides.

Statement E is true as the by-products formed are $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$, $\text{NaBr}$, and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

Therefore, only statements C and E are true.

Answer: C and E only

q: Which of the following postulate of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom in not in agreement with quantum mechanical model of an atom ?
question: Which of the following postulate of Bohr's model of hydrogen atom in not in agreement with quantum mechanical model of an atom ?
options: An atom in a stationary state does not emit electromagnetic radiation as long as it stays in the same state
An atom can take only certain distinct energies $\mathrm{E}_1, \mathrm{E}_2, \mathrm{E}_3$, etc. These allowed states of constant energy are called the stationary states of atom
When an electron makes a transition from a higher energy stationary state to a lower energy stationary state, then it emits a photon of light
The electron in a H atom's stationary state moves in a circle around the nucleus
solution: The electron in a H-atom's stationary state moves in a spherical path.

q:
Consider the following data:
$\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ (methane, g$)=-\mathrm{X} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite $=\mathrm{Y} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\mathrm{Z} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
The bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is given by :
question:
Consider the following data:
$\Delta_{f} \mathrm{H}^{\ominus}$ (methane, g$)=-\mathrm{X} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite $=\mathrm{Y} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{H}_{2}=\mathrm{Z} \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}{ }^{-1}$
The bond enthalpy of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond is given by :
solution:
For methane formation: $\text{C(g)} + 2\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g)$.
Using enthalpy balance: $\Delta_f H° = \text{Bonds broken} - \text{Bonds formed}$.
Breaking: sublimation of C (Y) and 2 H-H bonds (2Z); Forming: 4 C-H bonds.
Energy equation: $-X = Y + 2Z - 4B$ where B is C-H bond enthalpy.
Solving: $4B = X + Y + 2Z$, so $B = \frac{X + Y + 2Z}{4}$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Match List-I with List-II

  List-I
(Complex)		   List-II
(Hybridization)
A NiCO4 I sp3
B NiCN42- II sp3d2
C CoCN63- III d2sp3
D CoF63- IV dsp2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Match List-I with List-II

  List-I
(Complex)		   List-II
(Hybridization)
A NiCO4 I sp3
B NiCN42- II sp3d2
C CoCN63- III d2sp3
D CoF63- IV dsp2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
Ni is in zero oxidation state in Ni(CO)4 so the electronic configuration of Ni is 3d84s2. As CO is a strong ligand, it pushes all the electrons in the 3d orbital, therefore the hybridisation of Ni(CO)4 is sp3 and it has tetrahedral geometry. It is diamagnetic due to the absence of unpaired electrons.

In [Ni(CN)4]2-, there is Ni2+ ion for which the electronic configuration in the valence shell is 3d84s0.

In presence of strong field CN- ions, all the electrons are paired up.

The empty, 3d, 3s and two 4p Orbitals undergo dsp2 hybridization to make bonds with CN- ligands in square planar geometry.

The atomic number of Co is 27 and its valence shell electronic configuration is 3d74s2.

Co is in +3 oxidation state in the complex [CoF6]3-.

[CoF6]3- is sp3d2 hybridized and it is octahedral in shape.

Another Co3+ complex, [Co(CN)6]3-, is diamagnetic and has no unpaired electrons. The hybrid orbitals used to form this complex are d2sp3.

NiCO4 Hybridisation sp3

NiCN42- Hybridisation dsp2

CoCN63- Hybridisation d2Sp3

CoF63- Hybridisation sp3d2

subject: Chemistry
q: The incorrect statement from the following for borazine is:
question: The incorrect statement from the following for borazine is:
solution:
The statement "It contains a banana bond" is incorrect about Borazine.

Borazine, also known as inorganic benzene or borazole, is a chemical compound with the formula B3N3H6. Borazine is a hexagonal planar molecule with alternating boron and nitrogen atoms, and it exhibits some structural and chemical similarities to benzene. Borazine is an aromatic compound with electron delocalization. It does not contain banana bond.

On heating, borazine hydrolyses slowly.B3 N3H6+9H2O→3NH3+3H3BO3+3H2O

q: Boiling point of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> aqueous solution of a nonvolatile solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is equal to the boiling point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>%</mo></math> aqueous solution of a non-volatile solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. The relation between molecular weights of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is.
question: Boiling point of a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>%</mo></math> aqueous solution of a nonvolatile solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is equal to the boiling point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>%</mo></math> aqueous solution of a non-volatile solute <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>. The relation between molecular weights of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is.
solution:
For A : 100gm solution →2gm solute A ∴ Molality =2/MA0.098

For B : 100gm solution →8gm solute B ∴ Molality =8/MB0.092

∵ ΔTBA=ΔTBB

∴ Molality of A= Molality of B

∴20.098MA=80.092MB

298×928=MAMB

14.261=MAMB

∴ MB=4.261×MA

subject: Chemistry
q:
Amongst the following the number of oxide(s) which are paramagnetic in nature is

Na2O,KO2,NO2, N2O,ClO2,NO,SO2,Cl2O
question:
Amongst the following the number of oxide(s) which are paramagnetic in nature is

Na2O,KO2,NO2, N2O,ClO2,NO,SO2,Cl2O
solution:
The magnetic properties of a substance can be determined by examining its electron configuration: If it has unpaired electrons, then the substance is paramagnetic and if all electrons are paired, the substance is then diamagnetic.

KO2 is potassium superoxide. There is one unpaired electron in anti-bonding molecular orbital of superoxide. Potassium superoxide is paramagnetic due to the presence of unpaired electron.

NO2,ClO2,NO are odd electron species. Hence, they are paramagnetic.

The other are monoxide and dioxide contain paired electrons, so they will show diamagnetic behaviour.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The 'spin only' magnetic moment value of \(\mathrm{MO}_4{ }^{2-}\) is _______ BM. (Where M is a metal having least metallic radii. among \(\mathrm{Sc}, \mathrm{Ti}, \mathrm{V}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Mn}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}\) ).
(Given atomic number: \(\mathrm{Sc}=21, \mathrm{Ti}=22, \mathrm{~V}=23, \mathrm{Cr}=24, \mathrm{Mn}=25\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}=30\))
question:
The 'spin only' magnetic moment value of \(\mathrm{MO}_4{ }^{2-}\) is _______ BM. (Where M is a metal having least metallic radii. among \(\mathrm{Sc}, \mathrm{Ti}, \mathrm{V}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Mn}\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}\) ).
(Given atomic number: \(\mathrm{Sc}=21, \mathrm{Ti}=22, \mathrm{~V}=23, \mathrm{Cr}=24, \mathrm{Mn}=25\) and \(\mathrm{Zn}=30\))
solution:
Metal having least metallic radii among $\mathrm{Sc}, \mathrm{Ti}, \mathrm{V}$, $\mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Mn} \& \mathrm{Zn}$ is $\mathrm{Cr}$.
Spin only magnetic moment of $\mathrm{CrO}_4^{2-}$.
Here $\mathrm{Cr}^{+6}$ is in $\mathrm{d}^0$ configuration (diamagnetic).

The vapour pressure of the final solution, the mole fractions of components <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> in the vapour phase, respectively are
question: The vapour pressures of pure liquids <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math>are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi><mo> </mo><mi>Hg</mi></math> respectively at <math><mn>298</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math>On mixing the two liquids, the sum of their volumes is equal to the volume of the final mixture. The mole fraction of liquid <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is <math><mn>0.5</mn></math> in the mixture. The vapour pressure of the final solution, the mole fractions of components <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> in the vapour phase, respectively are
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.5</mn></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>450</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.6</mn></math>

<math><mn>450</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.5</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.5</mn></math>

<math><mn>500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.4</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>0.6</mn></math>
solution:
Raoult’s law for ideal solution,

PTotal=PA+ PB

PTotal=xA×PA∘+ xB×PB∘

PTotal=12×400+12×600=500 mm of Hg

Dalton’s law,

yA×PTotal=PA 

yA=xA×PA0PTotal=12×400500=0.4

yB=1-0.4=0.6
